_meta.authoringNotes: Anatomy and biomechanics per Espinosa et al. Clin Sports Med 2010 (PMID 20670866). Classification per Maceira and Monteagudo 2014 (PMID 24887010). Treatment per Yu et al. Foot Ankle Clin 2019. Plantar pressure mapping evidence per Burns et al. JFAR 2016. Imaging guidance per ESSR foot consensus 2022.

h1Title: Metatarsalgia: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Metatarsalgia is pain and inflammation in the ball of the foot, specifically the plantar surface of the metatarsal heads, that affects roughly 80 per 1,000 adults at some point and disproportionately impacts runners, dancers, and women who wear high-heeled or narrow-toed shoes. Pain typically localizes under the second, third, and fourth metatarsal heads and worsens during weight-bearing, push-off, and barefoot walking on hard floors. The condition is mechanical, not a single disease — it reflects altered load distribution across the forefoot from causes ranging from a long second metatarsal, hallux rigidus, claw or hammer toes, plantar plate tear, Morton's neuroma, Freiberg's avascular necrosis, and rheumatoid arthritis. Most cases improve within 8-12 weeks with footwear modification, metatarsal pads, custom orthotics, calf stretching, and short-course oral or topical NSAIDs; surgery is reserved for refractory mechanical causes such as plantar plate rupture or fixed toe deformities.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 80 per 1,000 adults experience metatarsalgia annually; prevalence rises to 10-15% in adults over 60
keyStats.demographics: Female-to-male ratio approximately 2-3:1, reflecting footwear choices and post-menopausal foot structural changes
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence age 50-70 years for mechanical causes; younger in runners, dancers, and athletes
keyStats.globalCases: Tens of millions of cases worldwide; one of the top three reasons for podiatric outpatient visits in most health systems

definition: Metatarsalgia (ICD-10: M77.40 unspecified foot; M77.41 right foot; M77.42 left foot) is a clinical syndrome of pain in the metatarsal region of the forefoot, specifically the plantar aspect of the second to fifth metatarsophalangeal joints. It is best understood as a final common pathway of multiple anatomical and biomechanical disorders that overload one or more metatarsal heads. Primary metatarsalgia arises from intrinsic foot anatomy: a long second metatarsal, plantarflexed metatarsals, hallux valgus, hallux rigidus, Morton's foot, equinus contracture, or cavus arch. Secondary metatarsalgia results from focal pathology: plantar plate tear, Morton's interdigital neuroma, Freiberg's avascular necrosis of the second metatarsal head, sesamoid disorders, rheumatoid arthritis with subluxation of the lesser metatarsophalangeal joints, gout, or stress fracture. Iatrogenic metatarsalgia follows hallux valgus surgery that shifts load laterally, prior amputation of a lesser ray, or post-traumatic deformity. Classification by Maceira and Monteagudo distinguishes propulsive metatarsalgia (excess load during push-off due to first ray insufficiency) from non-propulsive metatarsalgia (overload during the contact phase from cavus foot or tight gastrocnemius). Podiatry leads diagnosis and conservative management; foot and ankle orthopedic surgeons address structural causes. Diagnosis combines history, biomechanical examination, footwear assessment, plantar pressure mapping when available, and selective imaging.

typesClassification.description: Caused by intrinsic foot anatomy: long second metatarsal, plantarflexed lesser metatarsals, Morton's foot configuration, cavus arch, hallux valgus, or hallux rigidus that transfers load to the lesser rays. Treatment addresses the underlying mechanics.

Results from focal lesions such as plantar plate tear, Morton's interdigital neuroma, Freiberg's disease, sesamoiditis, intermetatarsal bursitis, rheumatoid arthritis with MTP subluxation, or stress fracture. Treatment targets the specific pathology.

Develops after first ray surgery (hallux valgus correction with shortening, hallux rigidus arthrodesis), amputation of a lesser ray, or post-traumatic deformity. Load is redistributed to remaining metatarsals, producing transfer metatarsalgia.
Excess load during push-off, typically due to first ray insufficiency (hallux valgus, dorsal escape of the first metatarsal, hallux rigidus). Pain peaks at toe-off in gait analysis.
Overload during the foot-flat contact phase, often due to cavus arch, tight gastrocnemius, or rigid forefoot. Pain peaks earlier in stance phase.

primarySymptoms: Burning, aching, or sharp pain under the ball of the foot, most commonly beneath the second, third, and fourth metatarsal heads.
Pain that worsens with weight-bearing, running, jumping, and barefoot walking on hard floors and improves with rest.
Sensation of walking on a pebble, marble, or fold of sock under the foot.
Numbness, tingling, or pins-and-needles between the third and fourth toes when Morton's neuroma is the cause.
Callus formation under the second or third metatarsal head reflecting chronic high-pressure loading.
Forefoot swelling or fullness in inflammatory or post-traumatic cases.
Clicking or popping sensation during heel rise, sometimes audible in plantar plate tears (Mulder click).
Pain on direct palpation of one or more metatarsal heads — the most consistent bedside finding.
Worsening pain with high-heeled, narrow-toed, or thin-soled shoes; relief in cushioned, stiff-soled footwear.
Hammer toe, claw toe, or crossover deformity of the second toe in chronic cases with plantar plate insufficiency.
earlyWarningSigns: New ball-of-foot pain after a recent increase in running mileage or new footwear
Discomfort during the push-off phase of walking after wearing high-heeled shoes
Recurring callus under one or more metatarsal heads despite regular callus removal
Sensation of catching or instability of the second toe (early plantar plate insufficiency)
Persistent burning between the third and fourth toes after long walks
emergencySigns: Sudden severe forefoot pain with inability to bear weight — exclude stress fracture, acute gout, or septic arthritis
Foot redness, swelling, warmth, and fever — possible cellulitis or septic arthritis requiring urgent assessment
Acute trauma with deformity or open wound — emergency department evaluation
Cold, pale, or pulseless toes — exclude critical limb ischemia
Rapidly progressive foot deformity in a person with diabetes or peripheral neuropathy — consider Charcot foot

causes.cause: Mechanical overload from foot structure and gait
causes.description: A long second metatarsal, plantarflexed metatarsals, cavus arch, equinus contracture, or first ray insufficiency redirects ground reaction forces to one or two metatarsal heads. Cumulative load produces capsular inflammation, bursitis, and eventual structural damage.

causes.cause: Footwear (high heels, narrow toe-box, thin soles)
causes.description: Heels above 2 inches shift up to 75% of body weight onto the forefoot. Narrow toe-boxes compress the metatarsals and toes, and thin soles transmit ground impact directly to the metatarsal heads. Footwear modification alone resolves up to 50% of cases.

causes.cause: Plantar plate tear of a lesser metatarsophalangeal joint
causes.description: The plantar plate is a fibrocartilaginous structure stabilizing the metatarsophalangeal joints. Acute or chronic rupture (most commonly the second MTP) produces dorsal subluxation of the toe, crossover deformity, and progressive forefoot pain. MRI or ultrasound confirms the diagnosis.

Fibrosis and entrapment of an interdigital nerve, most often between the third and fourth metatarsal heads. Produces burning, electric-shock pain radiating into the toes and is reproduced by a palpable Mulder click on transverse compression.

Idiopathic osteonecrosis of the second metatarsal head, most common in adolescent girls and young women, producing focal metatarsalgia with joint stiffness. Imaging shows flattening and fragmentation of the metatarsal head.

causes.cause: Rheumatoid arthritis, gout, and inflammatory arthropathy
causes.description: Chronic synovitis erodes the metatarsophalangeal joints, leading to subluxation, hammer toes, and severe metatarsalgia. Up to 90% of long-standing rheumatoid arthritis patients develop forefoot deformity.

Common in runners, dancers, and military recruits after sudden increases in activity. The second or third metatarsal is most commonly affected. Pain is point-tender, worse on weight-bearing, and confirmed by MRI or repeat radiograph after 2-3 weeks.

riskFactors.description: Women have 2-3 fold higher incidence than men, reflecting both footwear choices (high heels, narrow-toed shoes) and post-menopausal foot pad atrophy.
Running, ballet, sprinting, basketball, and gymnastics produce repetitive forefoot loading. Mileage increases above 10% per week or hard-surface running double the incidence.

Long second metatarsal (Morton's foot), plantarflexed metatarsals, hallux valgus, hallux rigidus, cavus arch, and equinus contracture all increase focal forefoot pressure. Identified on examination and plantar pressure mapping.
Each inch of heel height shifts roughly 22% of body weight forward; 3-inch heels concentrate 75% of weight on the forefoot. Daily wear for years contributes to chronic forefoot overload.
Obesity proportionally increases ground reaction forces on the forefoot. Weight reduction of 5-10% measurably decreases plantar pressures and pain scores.

riskFactors.factor: Age over 50 with plantar fat pad atrophy
riskFactors.description: Plantar fat pad thickness reduces by approximately 25% between ages 40 and 70, decreasing natural cushioning of the metatarsal heads.
Inflammatory arthropathy progressively destroys the metatarsophalangeal joints and is one of the strongest predictors of severe metatarsalgia in older adults.

affectedDemographics: Roughly 80 per 1,000 adults annually; one of the top three reasons for podiatric consultation
Female predominance 2-3:1, partly attributable to footwear
Older adults (over 60) have 10-15% point prevalence; fat pad atrophy and arthritis are major drivers
Runners, dancers, and military recruits have 5-10 fold higher incidence than sedentary peers
Rheumatoid arthritis patients: up to 90% develop forefoot deformity and metatarsalgia over time
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is primarily clinical, supported by selective imaging when the cause is unclear or surgery is being considered. The history asks about location, character (sharp, burning, aching), aggravating activities, footwear pattern, prior trauma, comorbid conditions (rheumatoid arthritis, diabetes, gout), and any systemic features. Examination begins with weight-bearing inspection of foot posture (cavus or flat arch, hallux valgus, hammer toes, crossover deformity), palpation of each metatarsal head for tenderness, the Mulder click maneuver for Morton's neuroma, the Lachman or vertical drawer test for plantar plate integrity, joint range of motion, and assessment of calf tightness (equinus). Plantar callosities are mapped — chronic callus under a metatarsal head indicates elevated pressure. Footwear is inspected for wear patterns, heel height, toe box width, and sole stiffness. Plain weight-bearing radiographs (AP, lateral, oblique) measure metatarsal lengths, identify Freiberg flattening, sesamoid alignment, stress fractures, and joint changes from inflammatory arthritis. Ultrasound is the first-line modality for Morton's neuroma, plantar plate tear, and intermetatarsal bursitis. MRI is reserved for difficult cases, plantar plate grading, suspected occult stress fracture, and pre-surgical planning. Plantar pressure mapping in shoe or barefoot identifies regions of focal high pressure and guides orthotic prescription. Blood tests (uric acid, rheumatoid factor, anti-CCP, ESR, CRP) are obtained when inflammatory arthropathy is suspected.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies bony causes — long second metatarsal, Freiberg flattening, stress fracture, joint erosion, alignment
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Three standard radiographs taken while standing. 10-15 minutes; results same day. Repeat at 2-3 weeks if a stress fracture is suspected and initial film is normal.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects Morton's neuroma, plantar plate tear, intermetatarsal bursitis, and joint effusion
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed in the podiatry or radiology department in 15-30 minutes. Dynamic assessment of the plantar plate and Mulder click adds value. Image-guided injection often combined.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reference standard for plantar plate tears, Freiberg necrosis, occult stress fracture, and soft-tissue tumors
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minute scan without contrast for most indications. Report within 24-48 hours. Specialist foot coils give the highest resolution.

diagnosticTests.test: Plantar pressure mapping (in-shoe sensors or barefoot platform)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies focal pressures under each metatarsal head, guides custom orthotic prescription, and assesses surgical results
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient walks along a sensor mat or with insole sensors for 10-15 minutes. Report identifies pressure peaks and force-time integrals.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms the painful structure when multiple causes coexist
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Ultrasound-guided injection of 1-2 mL lidocaine to the suspected site. Marked pain relief within minutes confirms the location of the lesion.

treatmentOverview: Treatment follows a stepwise plan beginning with footwear modification, mechanical offloading, and conservative therapy, escalating to procedures and surgery only when these fail. Footwear changes are first-line: cushioned shoes with stiff soles, wide toe-boxes, heel heights under 2 inches, and rocker-bottom outsoles offload the forefoot in 50-70% of patients within 6-8 weeks. Metatarsal pads placed just proximal to the painful metatarsal head redistribute pressure and reduce focal loading by 15-25% on plantar pressure measurement. Custom-molded orthotics with metatarsal domes are prescribed when off-the-shelf options are inadequate. Calf and intrinsic foot stretching addresses equinus contracture and tight gastrocnemius, a common contributor. Short courses of oral NSAIDs or topical diclofenac control inflammation; paracetamol is first-line in older adults with cardiovascular or renal risk. Intra-articular or peri-neural corticosteroid injection (triamcinolone 20-40 mg) is effective for refractory Morton's neuroma, intermetatarsal bursitis, and inflammatory metatarsalgia, with response in 60-75% lasting 3-6 months. Plantar plate tears that are mild and stable usually respond to taping, orthotics, and a stiff carbon plate insert. Severe or unstable tears, fixed hammer toe deformities, Freiberg disease with persistent symptoms, and rheumatoid forefoot deformity may require surgery: Weil osteotomy of the metatarsal to shorten and elevate the metatarsal head, plantar plate repair, hammer toe correction with arthrodesis or arthroplasty, Freiberg joint debridement and osteotomy, or rheumatoid forefoot reconstruction with first MTP arthrodesis and lesser toe correction. Postoperative rehabilitation includes 4-6 weeks of forefoot-offloading shoe followed by graduated return to walking. Comprehensive management always addresses contributing factors — weight, footwear habits, sport-training errors — to prevent recurrence.

medicalTreatments.name: Footwear modification (cushioned, wide toe-box, stiff sole, heel under 2 inches, rocker outsole)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line treatment that resolves or substantially reduces symptoms in 50-70% of patients within 6-8 weeks. Stop high heels and narrow shoes; choose running shoes with adequate forefoot cushioning.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 50-70% improvement in pain scores at 8 weeks in observational cohorts

medicalTreatments.name: Metatarsal pad or dome placed just proximal to the painful metatarsal head
medicalTreatments.description: Inexpensive, immediate intervention that redistributes plantar pressure. Self-adhesive felt or gel pads can be tried before custom devices.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Plantar pressure reduction of 15-25% under the painful metatarsal head; pain improvement in 60-75%

medicalTreatments.name: Custom-molded foot orthotics with metatarsal dome
medicalTreatments.description: Prescription orthotics designed using plantar pressure mapping to offload the painful region. Replaced every 12-24 months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 60-80% achieve sustained symptom relief at 12 months when combined with footwear changes

medicalTreatments.name: Oral and topical NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400-600 mg three times daily, naproxen 500 mg twice daily, topical diclofenac)
medicalTreatments.description: Short-term symptom control for inflammatory and mechanical pain. Topical formulations preferred for older patients to limit systemic risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain reduction of 30-50% during 2-4 week courses

medicalTreatments.name: Corticosteroid injection (triamcinolone 20-40 mg with local anesthetic)
medicalTreatments.description: Ultrasound-guided injection for Morton's neuroma, intermetatarsal bursitis, plantar plate inflammation, or inflammatory MTP synovitis. Limit to 2-3 injections per joint per year.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom relief in 60-75% lasting 3-6 months; cure of Morton's neuroma in 30-40%

medicalTreatments.name: Calf stretching and intrinsic foot exercises
medicalTreatments.description: Daily stretching of gastrocnemius and soleus complemented by toe-spread and short-foot exercises to strengthen intrinsic muscles. 10-15 minutes twice daily.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain reduction of 20-30% over 12 weeks; reduces recurrence by addressing equinus contracture

surgicalOptions.name: Weil osteotomy of the lesser metatarsal
surgicalOptions.description: Distal oblique osteotomy that shortens and elevates the metatarsal head, redistributing plantar pressure. Performed for primary mechanical metatarsalgia, plantar plate insufficiency, and Freiberg disease.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Pain relief in 80-90% at 2 years; floating toe (transfer dorsally) in 10-15%

surgicalOptions.name: Plantar plate repair (direct suture or pulley reconstruction)
surgicalOptions.description: Restores second MTP joint stability for tears that have failed conservative management. Often combined with Weil osteotomy and hammer toe correction.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Pain relief in 75-85% at 2 years; recurrence of crossover toe in 10-20%

surgicalOptions.name: Hammer toe correction (PIP arthrodesis or arthroplasty)
surgicalOptions.description: Fuses or resects the proximal interphalangeal joint to straighten the toe and reduce plantar pressure on the metatarsal head.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic improvement in 80-90%; nonunion in 5-10% of arthrodesis cases

surgicalOptions.name: Freiberg disease joint debridement and dorsal closing wedge osteotomy
surgicalOptions.description: Removes necrotic bone and redirects healthy cartilage into the joint contact area. Performed in advanced Freiberg with persistent symptoms.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic improvement in 80-90% in selected cases at 5 years

surgicalOptions.name: Rheumatoid forefoot reconstruction
surgicalOptions.description: First MTP arthrodesis with lesser metatarsal head resection or osteotomy and lesser toe correction. Restores stable, plantigrade forefoot in advanced rheumatoid deformity.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Patient-reported improvement in 80-90% of advanced rheumatoid forefoot cases; revision rate 10-15%

alternativeTreatments.name: Extracorporeal shockwave therapy (ESWT)
alternativeTreatments.description: Investigational for Morton's neuroma and recalcitrant metatarsalgia; small randomized trials show modest benefit at 3 months. Not yet standard care.

alternativeTreatments.name: Cryoneurolysis or radiofrequency ablation for Morton's neuroma
alternativeTreatments.description: Image-guided minimally invasive procedures used in refractory neuroma cases. Symptom relief in 60-80% at 6-12 months in case series; less invasive than neurectomy.

whySeeSpecialist: Patients with metatarsalgia that fails to improve after 6-8 weeks of footwear modification and self-care, or with mechanical signs (deformity, crossover toe, instability), should see a podiatrist or foot and ankle surgeon. Earlier specialist input is needed for athletes, runners with suspected stress fracture, diabetic patients, and patients with known inflammatory arthritis to prevent progression.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a podiatrist or foot and ankle orthopedic surgeon with access to plantar pressure mapping, ultrasound, and a custom orthotics laboratory. Surgical treatment should be performed by a fellowship-trained foot and ankle surgeon at a center performing more than 50 forefoot procedures annually.
hospitalCriteria: Podiatry and foot and ankle orthopedic services on site
Ultrasound and MRI facilities for forefoot imaging
Custom orthotics laboratory or partnership with an external prosthetics provider
Multidisciplinary rheumatoid arthritis foot clinic where relevant
keyFacilities: Sports medicine and gait analysis labs
preventionStrategies: Wear cushioned shoes with wide toe-boxes and heel heights under 2 inches; rotate footwear to avoid prolonged identical loading.
Replace running shoes every 500-800 km; worn midsoles transmit increased forefoot pressure.
Increase running mileage by no more than 10% per week to allow tissue adaptation.
Stretch the calves daily for 5-10 minutes; tight gastrocnemius is a major contributor.
Maintain BMI under 30 kg/m² to reduce ground reaction forces on the forefoot.
Inspect feet daily for callus formation and address chronic high-pressure zones with metatarsal pads or orthotics.
lifestyleModifications: Wear prescribed orthotics in every weight-bearing shoe, including indoors.
Avoid prolonged walking or standing on hard surfaces during the recovery phase.
Replace high-impact activities (running, jumping) with low-impact alternatives (swimming, cycling) until symptoms settle.
Use metatarsal pads in athletic and casual shoes; replace pads every 3-6 months.
Cool the forefoot for 15-20 minutes after extended weight-bearing activity in the first 4-6 weeks of treatment.
Maintain regular calf, hamstring, and intrinsic foot stretching as part of daily routine.

recommended: Mediterranean-style diet with whole grains, vegetables, fruits, oily fish, and olive oil to reduce systemic inflammation
Adequate protein (1-1.2 g/kg/day) to support tissue repair, particularly after surgery
Calcium and vitamin D for bone health, especially in stress fracture risk
Hydration with at least 1.5-2 L of water daily
avoid: Excess alcohol that impairs tendon and connective tissue healing
Sugar-sweetened beverages and refined carbohydrates that promote weight gain
Foods high in purines (organ meat, anchovies, beer) if gout is the underlying cause
Smoking, which impairs bone and soft-tissue healing

exerciseGuidelines: Substitute low-impact aerobic activity (swimming, stationary cycling, elliptical) during the active phase. Progressive return to walking and jogging guided by symptom resolution and orthotic use. Maintain calf and hamstring flexibility and intrinsic foot strengthening throughout recovery and long-term prevention.
dailyManagement: Inspect both feet daily for callus formation and skin breakdown.
Wear prescribed orthotics in every shoe, including indoors.
Perform daily calf and intrinsic foot stretching for 10-15 minutes.
Apply ice to the forefoot after extended walking, especially in the first 4-6 weeks.
Track pain scores and aggravating activities in a simple diary to inform podiatry follow-up.
Attend podiatry and rehabilitation appointments at the scheduled intervals.
prognosis: With structured conservative management, 70-85% of patients achieve substantial symptom relief within 12 weeks. Mechanical metatarsalgia from footwear and minor structural variations responds best, often with footwear changes and metatarsal pads alone. Plantar plate tears with crossover deformity, advanced Freiberg disease, and rheumatoid forefoot deformity have higher rates of progression and may require surgery; surgical outcomes for these indications are favorable, with 80-90% pain relief at 2 years. Recurrence within 5 years is common when underlying contributors (footwear, occupational standing, weight, equinus) are not addressed. In diabetic patients with neuropathy and metatarsalgia, vigilant offloading prevents progression to ulceration and Charcot foot. Athletes typically return to full sport within 3-6 months with appropriate rehabilitation; runners with biomechanical contributors may need permanent orthotic use.
recoveryTimeline: Footwear modification and metatarsal pads: 6-8 weeks for noticeable improvement. Custom orthotics: 8-12 weeks for full benefit after the initial adaptation period. Corticosteroid injection: relief within 1-2 weeks, lasting 3-6 months. Surgical recovery: 4-6 weeks in postoperative shoe, return to regular shoes by 8-12 weeks, full activity at 4-6 months.
complications: Chronic plantar callosity with hyperkeratosis and risk of skin breakdown over the metatarsal heads; debridement and offloading prevent progression.
Plantar plate rupture progressing to crossover or hammer toe deformity if undertreated.
Stress fracture of an adjacent metatarsal after transfer of load (especially after first ray surgery or amputation).
Joint subluxation and dislocation in inflammatory arthritis affecting the metatarsophalangeal joints.
Foot ulceration and Charcot foot in diabetic patients with combined neuropathy and metatarsalgia.
Recurrence after surgery in 10-20%, particularly when underlying contributors are not corrected.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons (AAOS) — Metatarsalgia
supportResources.description: Patient guide on metatarsalgia symptoms, causes, and treatment options from the AAOS.

supportResources.name: American College of Foot and Ankle Surgeons (ACFAS) — Foot pain conditions
supportResources.description: Foot and ankle surgical society patient education on metatarsalgia and related forefoot conditions.

supportResources.name: American Podiatric Medical Association
supportResources.description: US podiatric association with patient resources and clinician directory.

supportResources.name: NICE Clinical Knowledge Summaries — Foot pain
supportResources.description: UK summary of evidence-based management of foot pain conditions including metatarsalgia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Morton's Neuroma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Morton's neuroma causes burning, electric pain radiating between the third and fourth toes with numbness and a positive Mulder click. Metatarsalgia is broader ball-of-foot pain often without nerve symptoms. The two often coexist and are distinguished by clinical examination and ultrasound.

confusedWithConditions.name: Plantar Fasciitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Plantar fasciitis causes heel pain at the medial calcaneal tubercle, worst on first steps in the morning and after rest. Metatarsalgia is forefoot pain under the metatarsal heads worsening with prolonged weight-bearing. Examination readily distinguishes the two.

confusedWithConditions.name: Gout
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Gout causes sudden severe pain, redness, warmth, and swelling, classically in the first metatarsophalangeal joint and resolves over 7-14 days. Metatarsalgia is chronic, mechanical, and lacks the dramatic acute inflammation of a gout flare.

confusedWithConditions.name: Osteoarthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Osteoarthritis of the great toe (hallux rigidus) causes pain and stiffness at the first MTP joint with restricted dorsiflexion, while metatarsalgia typically affects the lesser MTP joints. The two can coexist when first ray rigidity transfers load laterally.

relatedConditions.name: Morton's Neuroma
relatedConditions.relevance: Common cause of forefoot pain that often coexists with mechanical metatarsalgia and shares treatment pathways.

relatedConditions.name: Plantar Fasciitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequently coexists due to shared biomechanical risk factors including equinus contracture; addressed together in rehabilitation.

relatedConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Long-term RA produces severe forefoot deformity and metatarsalgia in up to 90% of patients; rheumatology and podiatry collaborate on management.

relatedConditions.name: Osteoarthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Hallux rigidus and lesser MTP osteoarthritis are common in older patients with metatarsalgia.

relatedConditions.name: Gout
relatedConditions.relevance: Differential diagnosis and a contributor to chronic forefoot pain in some patients.

faqs.question: What is metatarsalgia in simple terms?
faqs.answer: Metatarsalgia is pain and inflammation in the ball of the foot, usually under the long bones (metatarsals) just behind the toes. It feels like walking on a pebble or marble and is worse with standing, walking on hard floors, and wearing high heels or thin-soled shoes.

faqs.question: What causes metatarsalgia?
faqs.answer: Common causes include footwear with high heels or narrow toe-boxes, foot structure variations such as a long second metatarsal or cavus arch, plantar plate tears, Morton's neuroma, Freiberg avascular necrosis, rheumatoid arthritis, and stress fractures. Many cases reflect mechanical overload rather than a single disease.

faqs.question: How is metatarsalgia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is based on history, examination of the forefoot, and weight-bearing radiographs. Ultrasound or MRI identifies plantar plate tears, Morton's neuroma, and stress fractures. Plantar pressure mapping guides custom orthotic prescription in chronic cases.

faqs.question: How is metatarsalgia treated?
faqs.answer: First-line treatment is footwear modification, metatarsal pads, custom orthotics, calf stretching, and short courses of NSAIDs. Corticosteroid injections help refractory inflammatory cases. Surgery is reserved for fixed deformities, plantar plate tears, and advanced Freiberg disease.

faqs.question: What are the best shoes for metatarsalgia?
faqs.answer: Cushioned shoes with stiff soles, wide toe-boxes, heel heights under 2 inches, and rocker-bottom outsoles offload the forefoot. Avoid high heels, narrow toe-boxes, and thin-soled flats. Athletic shoes designed for stability and forefoot cushioning are good day-to-day options.

faqs.question: Will metatarsalgia go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Mild mechanical metatarsalgia often improves within 4-8 weeks with rest, footwear changes, and metatarsal pads. Persistent or severe pain requires structured treatment. Structural causes (long second metatarsal, hallux rigidus) often persist without intervention.

faqs.question: Are metatarsal pads effective?
faqs.answer: Yes. A metatarsal pad placed just proximal to the painful metatarsal head reduces plantar pressure by 15-25% in pressure mapping studies and provides symptom relief in 60-75% of patients. Pads can be tried before custom orthotics.

faqs.question: Can high heels cause metatarsalgia?
faqs.answer: Yes. High heels shift up to 75% of body weight onto the forefoot, increasing pressure under the metatarsal heads. Chronic wear contributes to metatarsalgia, hallux valgus, hammer toes, and Morton's neuroma. Limiting heel height to under 2 inches reduces forefoot pressure substantially.

faqs.question: How long does metatarsalgia last?
faqs.answer: With proper management, most cases improve within 6-12 weeks. Chronic cases due to structural issues or inflammatory arthritis may require ongoing orthotic use and periodic treatment. Surgical recovery for plantar plate repair or osteotomy takes 4-6 months.

faqs.question: Is metatarsalgia the same as Morton's neuroma?
faqs.answer: No. Morton's neuroma is a specific cause of metatarsalgia involving fibrosis and entrapment of an interdigital nerve, producing burning pain and numbness between the toes. Metatarsalgia is broader, including mechanical, structural, and inflammatory causes. The two often coexist.

faqs.question: Does running cause metatarsalgia?
faqs.answer: Running can cause metatarsalgia, particularly with sudden mileage increases, worn shoes, hard-surface training, or biomechanical contributors. Stress fractures of the metatarsals are a particular concern. Progressive training, shoe rotation, and proper recovery reduce risk.

faqs.question: Are corticosteroid injections safe for metatarsalgia?
faqs.answer: Ultrasound-guided corticosteroid injections are effective for inflammatory metatarsalgia, Morton's neuroma, intermetatarsal bursitis, and joint synovitis. Limit to 2-3 injections per site per year because of risks of fat pad atrophy and plantar plate weakening with repeated injection.

faqs.question: What is a Weil osteotomy?
faqs.answer: A Weil osteotomy is a surgical procedure that shortens and elevates the lesser metatarsal head to redistribute plantar pressure. It is used for refractory primary mechanical metatarsalgia, plantar plate insufficiency, and Freiberg disease, with pain relief in 80-90% at 2 years.

faqs.question: Can metatarsalgia cause toe deformities?
faqs.answer: Yes. Untreated plantar plate tears progress to dorsal subluxation of the second MTP joint and crossover or hammer toe deformity. Chronic metatarsalgia from rheumatoid arthritis or hallux valgus also produces lesser toe deformities through joint instability.

faqs.question: Is surgery often needed for metatarsalgia?
faqs.answer: No. The majority of patients respond to footwear modification, orthotics, stretching, NSAIDs, and selective injections. Surgery is reserved for fixed mechanical deformities, severe plantar plate tears, advanced Freiberg disease, and rheumatoid forefoot reconstruction.

faqs.question: What is the difference between metatarsalgia and plantar fasciitis?
faqs.answer: Plantar fasciitis causes pain at the heel that is worst with the first steps in the morning. Metatarsalgia causes pain at the ball of the foot that worsens with prolonged weight-bearing. Examination at different sites of the foot readily distinguishes the two.

faqs.question: Can metatarsalgia affect both feet?
faqs.answer: Yes. Metatarsalgia is bilateral in many cases when caused by footwear, generalized inflammatory arthritis, or systemic biomechanical issues. Unilateral pain points more toward focal pathology such as a single plantar plate tear, neuroma, or stress fracture.

faqs.question: Why does metatarsalgia hurt more in older adults?
faqs.answer: The plantar fat pad thins by approximately 25% between ages 40 and 70, reducing natural cushioning under the metatarsal heads. Combined with arthritis, weakened intrinsic muscles, and chronic footwear effects, this produces higher rates of metatarsalgia in older adults.

faqs.question: Can weight loss help metatarsalgia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Weight loss of 5-10% measurably reduces plantar pressures and pain scores in patients with BMI over 30. Combined with footwear modification and orthotics, weight reduction is a powerful, durable intervention for mechanical metatarsalgia.

faqs.question: Do I need orthotics for life?
faqs.answer: Patients with persistent structural causes (cavus arch, long second metatarsal, hallux rigidus, post-surgical anatomy) typically benefit from long-term orthotic use. Patients whose metatarsalgia resolves with conservative care may discontinue orthotics, although recurrence is common without footwear discipline.

faqs.question: What exercises help metatarsalgia?
faqs.answer: Effective exercises include calf and gastrocnemius stretching, intrinsic foot exercises (toe spread, short-foot exercise, towel scrunches), ankle mobilization, and progressive forefoot strengthening. Performed daily for 10-15 minutes, they reduce pain by 20-30% over 12 weeks.

metaDescription: Metatarsalgia causes ball-of-foot pain from forefoot overload. Symptoms, footwear, orthotics, injections, surgery and prognosis, medically reviewed.
